hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s.

context.street_character: One-way NB | 30 km/h | Sub-standard sidewalks | Primary commercial spine | Pedestrian Sunday route

assessment.signage: Tin Sing International Trading Inc. bilingual Chinese/English signage; Melo's Wholesale/Retail on adjacent unit

photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Facade is occupied and readable from the street, with contemporary storefront alterations layered onto the earlier building form.
photo_observations.cornice_details: Simple fascia and coping with limited decorative trim visible.
photo_observations.chimney_details: No chimney clearly visible from the selected frontage view.
photo_observations.decorative_details: Character comes primarily from surviving massing, cladding, and current storefront treatment.
photo_observations.storefront_description: Two narrow storefronts: one shuttered/barred import shop and one dark bar frontage with large window.
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Storefront and signage have been updated from the original historic configuration.
photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: red-brown brick row with white painted lower infill at 157 and dark bar frontage at 159
photo_observations.window_details: Paired upper windows across both storefronts with heavily altered ground-floor shopfronts below.
photo_observations.door_details: Two doors at the white-barred Tin Sing frontage and one white side door at Handlebar beside the bar entry.
photo_observations.signage_observed: Tin Sing International Trading Inc. sign and Handlebar lettering.

storefront_description: Two narrow storefronts: one shuttered/barred import shop and one dark bar frontage with large window.

facade_description: 157 Augusta Ave presents a bay-and-gable domestic facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 4, 4 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 3 principal entrances. Character-defining facade elements include cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s.
